title: Honor and Hire Veterans Act of 2025
short_title: Honor and Hire Veterans Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Honor and Hire Veterans Act of 2025 makes it cheaper for businesses to hire veterans by giving them bigger tax credits. Right now, companies get a tax credit worth forty percent of what they pay a veteran in their first year of work. This bill increases that credit to fifty percent and also raises the limit on how much salary can count toward the credit. The new, bigger tax credits will only apply to veterans who get hired after this law starts. This should encourage more businesses to hire veterans by making it cost less to bring them on as employees.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Honor and Hire Veterans Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Work Opportunity Credit for Veterans
sections.text: This section changes the tax rules to give businesses bigger tax breaks when they hire veterans. Under current tax law, businesses can get a tax credit (money off their taxes) when they hire certain workers. This law makes that credit better for veterans. For veterans, businesses can now get back fifty percent of the wages they pay in the first year, up from the current forty percent. For all other workers in the program, the credit stays at forty percent. The law also raises the wage limits that count toward this credit. For most veterans, the limit goes from twelve thousand dollars to eighteen thousand dollars. For veterans with service connected disabilities (injuries or illnesses caused by military service), the limit goes from fourteen thousand dollars to twenty one thousand dollars. For long term unemployed veterans, the limit goes from twenty four thousand dollars to thirty six thousand dollars. If a worker does not stay employed long enough to meet the minimum work requirements, the credit percentages are cut in half. For veterans, it drops to twenty five percent instead of fifty percent. For other workers, it drops to twenty five percent instead of forty percent. These changes apply to any worker who starts their job after this law is signed.
